coredata.dc:title: A newly synthesized boronic acid-functionalized sulfur-doped carbon dot chemosensor as a molecular probe for glucose sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Othman, Hazha Omar
Hassan, Rebwar Omar
Faizullah, Azad Tawfiq
coredata.dc:description: The drawbacks of using an enzymatic assay as a traditional protocol for glucose include cost, time consumption, instability, and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Recently, glucose sensing with the aid of boronic acid-based molecules and their derivatives has been developed as an alternative tool to commonly used methods. In this work, a simple single-step hydrothermal method was effectively applied to synthetized boronic acid-functionalized sulfur-doped carbon nanodots (BS-CDs) using 3-thiophenylboronic acid as the starting material. In comparison with the common two-step synthetization of nanoparticle-based sensors, this synthesis procedure is less demanding and equally as effective in terms of nanofabrication and quantum yield 22.63%. The present sensing method arises from the assembly and fluorescence quenching of fluorescence nanoparticles (BS-CDs) when the glucose solution was added. The fluorescence intensity was recorded and applied to selectively estimate glucose in the concentration range of 1–250 μM with a detection limit of 0.57 μM. Fluorescence nanoparticles with boronic acid had excellent selectivity and strongly resisted interference from several biomolecules. The developed chemosensor was successfully applied in the measurement of glucose in human saliva.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boronic acid
Chemosensor
Carbon dots
Saliva

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of pH and incubation time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram describe synthesis procedure and application principle of BS-CD for glucose sensing.

Structure and component characterizations of fabricated BS-CD by: a) HR-TEM image with size distribution graph inset. b) FT-IR spectrum, c) UV–Vis. absorption and fluorescence spectrum, d) The fluorescence spectrum at different excitation wavelengths, and e) EDX spectrum.
Optimizations of the effective parameters (Fluorescence intensities of the BS-CD Fo alone and F in the presence of glucose): (a) pH influence. (b) Incubation time.
Fluorescence quenching spectra at different glucose concentrations and the TEM inset picture shows the large assembly formed due to reaction of BS-CS with Glucose.
Study of different interferences effects on the glucose determinations by the BS-CDs sensing method.
The analytical feature comparison for glucose detection of the current BS-CDs method with some previously published nano-sensors.
Application results of the present sensing system for glucose determination in saliva.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Method 1: glucose oxidase method using glucose oxidase kit GOD-POD.
Method 2: present sensing method by BS-CD ± SD (standard deviation), average of three replicates (n = 3).
*Glucose concentration (µM) determination after spiking (Method 2 + spiked amount) ± SD (standard deviation), average of three replicates (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A newly synthesized boronic acid-functionalized sulfur-doped carbon dot chemosensor as a molecular probe for glucose sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science, Salahaddin University, Erbil, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, College of Science, Salahaddin University, Erbil, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The drawbacks of using an enzymatic assay as a traditional protocol for glucose include cost, time consumption, instability, and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Recently, glucose sensing with the aid of boronic acid-based molecules and their derivatives has been developed as an alternative tool to commonly used methods. In this work, a simple single-step hydrothermal method was effectively applied to synthetized boronic acid-functionalized sulfur-doped carbon nanodots (BS-CDs) using 3-thiophenylboronic acid as the starting material. In comparison with the common two-step synthetization of nanoparticle-based sensors, this synthesis procedure is less demanding and equally as effective in terms of nanofabrication and quantum yield 22.63%. The present sensing method arises from the assembly and fluorescence quenching of fluorescence nanoparticles (BS-CDs) when the glucose solution was added. The fluorescence intensity was recorded and applied to selectively estimate glucose in the concentration range of 1–250 μM with a detection limit of 0.57 μM. Fluorescence nanoparticles with boronic acid had excellent selectivity and strongly resisted interference from several biomolecules. The developed chemosensor was successfully applied in the measurement of glucose in human saliva.

ce:section.ce:para: The principle source of energy in cellular metabolism is glucose, which plays a critical role in normal cell growth. Cellular functions are dangerously affected by the lack or excessive intake of glucose [1]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has illustrated that diabetes causes heart infections, kidney failure and intense immune disorders and has predicted that diabetes will be the 7th leading cause of death in the 2030s [2]. The most important step in treating diabetes is to control blood sugar levels [3]. Thus, a daily blood glucose level check and detection are essential for diabetes care and a necessary part of disease therapy [4]. Constant monitoring of glucose levels in the blood instructs the patient to know the amount of insulin to be injected according to the level of severity of the disease [5–7].

The enzyme immobilization strategy for glucose sensing is still a widely utilized method, although it suffers some challenges, including enzyme denaturation, thermal instability, and high variation of results caused by the oxygen concentration in the blood [8–11]. With the increased incidence rate of diabetes, it is important to develop appropriate methods for glucose estimation in terms of cost, speed, efficiency, easy, stability and availability. This situation has encouraged researchers to seek and develop a nonenzymatic-based sensor. New substances and methods not only significantly improve the categories of glucose sensor probes but also efficiently reduce the uncertainty of enzyme-based sensors [12].

Detection of an analyte in the blood usually suffers from some limitations; therefore, great attention has been paid to the use of other biologic fluids for disease diagnosis. Saliva is a fluid that offers several considerable advantages, including ease of sample collection without hurting the person, lack of need of specialized tools, economic favourability, and potential value for children and older adults since the collection of saliva is associated with fewer compliance problems than the collection of blood [13].

Monosaccharide molecules can be detected sensitively and selectively by boronic acid organic compound derivatives as they can covalently bind to a cis-diol fraction in a basic medium. The formation of the cis-diol structure complex depends on the thermodynamic characteristics of the boronic acid derivatives [14]. Boronic acid derivatives provide a beneficial method for the development of sensing probes for monosaccharaides [15,16]. The conjugation of boronic acid to any fluorophore causes significant fluorescence intensity changes upon binding to glucose in the form of changes in quenching time, intensity, and polarization [17–20]. Recently, researchers have given attention to carbon nanodots, which are novel members of the carbon nanofamily, mainly due to their strong fluorescence, tunable excitation and emission, water solubility, attractive photostability, and biocompatibility characteristics [21–26]. Carbon nanodots are encouraging candidates in sensing [27–32], catalysis [33,34], bioimaging [35–37] and light-emitting devices [38]. Moreover, carbon nanodots can easily be prepared via numerous simple, green, and low-cost procedures and are appropriate for various biosensing applications [39].

In this study, we describe an easy nonenzymatic fabrication strategy for sensing glucose in saliva. The chemosensor for this particular study was designed using a single-step synthesis procedure of boron, sulfur-doped carbon dots BS-CDs. 3-thiophenylboronic acid was chosen as the precursor due to its great harmony towards the π-electron cloud-rich carbon dot core. More emissive sites with strong selectivity for glucose sensing were created after functionalization of the sulfur-doped carbon dots with boronic acid due to the interaction between boronic acids and the cis-diol-group of glucose (Fig. 1 ) [40,41]. This fluorescence nanosensing method was sensitive, making it possible to differentiate glucose completely from both its analogue and other potential saliva interferences. No costly enzymes or complicated methods for modifying the surface are required. The fabricated chemosensor has a strong ability to measure glucose levels in saliva.

3-Thiophenylboronic acid (95%), Glucose, fructose, sucrose, lactose, and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification, and deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Absorbance spectra were recorded using a Lambda 25-UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, US). Fluorescence measurements were carried out by a Perkin Elmer LS- 45 fluorescence spectrometer (Buckinghamshire, UK). Characterization of the synthesized BS-CDs was performed using HR-TEM (PHILIPS, CM120, the Netherlands), SEM and an EDS TESCAN MIRA III (Czech). FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Perkin–Elmer spectrometer at room temperature.

A previously reported hydrothermal synthesis method was applied to prepare BS-CDs with few modifications [42,43]. Briefly, 1.6 g of 3-thiophenylboronic acid was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, followed by 30 min of stirring. The prepared solution was transferred to a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave, heated to 180 ± 3 °C for 6 h and then left to cool to room temperature. The solution containing BS-CDs was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The resulting supernatant was dialysed in a dialysis bag for 24 h. Purified BS-CDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The samples were prepared following the saliva collection protocol described previously by other researchers [44,45], with some adjustments, as follows:
1.
Wait for 2 min after rinsing the mouth with water.
2.
Minimize swallowing and hold saliva in the mouth (typically < 1 min).
3.
Place a dental sterilized cotton sponge in mouth and chew until it is soaked with saliva (typically < 1 min).
4.
Deposit the sponge into a syringe directly from the mouth without touching it to avoid contamination.
5.
Insert a plunger into the syringe.
6.
Gently squeeze the saliva into sterilized centrifuge tubes.
7.
Centrifuge the samples at 3500 rpm for 5 min.
8.
Perform glucose sensing with the clear supernatant (1 mL).

The quantum yield (
É ¸
) of synthesized BS-CDs was reported by measuring integrated photoluminescence intensities and absorbency values of the BS-CDs and comparing it with that obtained from quinine sulphate (QS) as a reference. The quinine sulphate (
É ¸
= 0.54 at 360 nm) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) while the BS-CDs was dissolved in ultra-pure water (η = 1.33). Then the quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
É ¸ =
É ¸
r
e
f
×
I
r
e
f
I
s
a
m
p
l
e
×
A
s
a
m
p
l
e
A
r
e
f
×
η
sample
η
ref
where
É ¸
is the quantum yield, I is measured integrated emission intensity (areas), η is the solvent refractive index, and A is the absorbance (the fraction of the light impinging on the sample that is absorbed), respectively. In the same experiment solutions, ηsample /ηref = 1.

The size and morphology of the synthesized BS-CDs were observed using HR-TEM. The image showed that the BS-CDs were well dispersed and spherical, with an average diameter of 4.5 nm and a lattice spacing distance of 0.23 nm. The lattice spacing explain both crystalline graphite and amorphous phases, which agrees with that of in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100). The standard deviation was 1.05 and the number of nanoparticles that used was 100 [46,47] (Fig. 2 a) while the inset graph shows the distribution of BS-CDs nanoparticle.

The surface structure and composition of the synthesized BS-CDs were determined by FTIR analysis (Fig. 2b). The obtained spectrum revealed many characteristic bands at 1310, 1191 and 1080 cm−1, which might be representative of B–O stretching, B–OH bending, and C–B stretching vibrational bands, respectively; these results agree with those from previous publications [48]. The broad band at approximately 3505 cm−1 in the FTIR curve was associated with the OH stretching vibration. Additionally, the peaks at 1701, 1469, and 687 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching, CH bending, and CS stretching vibrations of BS-CDs, respectively. These results demonstrate that boronic acid successfully functionalizes the prepared BS-CDs surfaces.

For further explanation and characterization of the prepared BS-CDs, the optical properties were investigated using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The absorption spectrum showed a strong peak at 245 nm for the π–π*(CC) transition, with a low intensity peak for the CO band appearing at 300–325 nm, corresponding to n–π*, and the extending edge band reached 450 nm due to π-conjugated bonds via the carbonization reaction, which is evidence of the existence of carbon dots and thus BS-CDs formation. Photoluminescence (PL) studies outline the dependence of the emission intensity on the excitation wavelength. The PL emission spectra in (Fig. 2d) show that the intensity increment with excitation wavelength reaches 360 nm and then gradually decreases. This excitation-dependent behaviour is one of the most critical properties of CDs. Thus, the as-prepared CDs had a maximum intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. This wavelength was selected and used in all subsequent experiments. Small redshifting was observed in the spectrum, which indicates less size dependence of the synthesized carbon dots [49–50].

Additionally, elemental analysis of the prepared nanoparticles was performed by energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 2e). The mass and atomic ratios of carbon (C), oxygen (O), boron (B) and sulfur (S) of the carbon dots were 53.10: 59.24: 42.75: 35.80 and 3.93: 4.88: 0.22: 0.09, respectively [51,52].

Generally, in the fabrication and design of any sensing system, two factors must be accounted for: First, specific receptors need to be able to conjugate with the analyte; second, as a result of this combination, an electrical or optical signal needs to be able to be detected with the high sensitivity and high selectivity. The designed strategy for glucose detection was based on static quenching of fluorescence emission. The proposed chemosensor is based on the formation of a boronate ester complex between boronic acid dispersed on the surfaces of the synthesized carbon dots BS-CDs and the cis-diol moieties of the chelated glucose. Interactions arose between 12.5 µM glucose (analyte) and boronic acid (receptor), leading to a change in the physicochemical properties of the fluorophore. Since each glucose molecule has two cis-diol sites, it can covalently bond with boronic acid-CDs; as a result, a large assembly formed. Formation of the BS-CDs-glucose assembly led to quenching of fluorescence, which is recognized as the chelation-enhanced quenching state (CHEQ) (Fig. 1) [53,54].

The stock solution was prepared by diluting 3.5 mL of synthesized and purified BS-CDs to a 100 mL volume using ultrapure water. This solution was kept protected from light and was stored for subsequent use. The BS-CDs stock solution (1.5 mL) was mixed thoroughly with different concentrations of a glucose solution (1–250 μM) in a series of 5 mL volumetric flasks. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.5 by using 0.1 M NaOH, and ultrapure water was used to achieve the desired volume. Fluorescence measurements at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm were performed after a 30-min period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of pH and incubation time
ce:section.ce:para: Chemically, the complexes of boronic acid and the vicinal diol of glucose have become a linchpin for the development of glucose sensing methods. Neutral boronic acid (sp2-trigonal) with an unoccupied p-orbital behaves as a Lewis acid. In aqueous solution, neutral boronic acid is able to interchange with boronate anions (conjugated base, sp3-tetrahedral) and become the main form of boron in neutral or slightly alkaline media. Upon addition of glucose, the boronate anions interact with the cis-diol moieties of glucose, forming cyclic boronate esters. This interaction causes an imbalance in the equilibrium between two boron states (boronic acid and boronate anions) and leads to the further dissolution of boronic acid and the formation of boronate anions. As a result, the addition of glucose leads to a decrease in the pKa value of boronic acid, as elucidated by Lorand and Edwards [55,56]. Boronic acid can also form a trigonal complex (Keq.trig), which is a stronger acid (K’a) than boronic acid, that is, K’a > Ka; consequently, Keq.tet > Keq.trig (Fig. S1). Thus, the overall reaction mechanism indicates that the acid-base interaction applies to the formation of boronate ester [57,58]. These findings lead us to conclude that the interactions of boronic acid with diols are highly dependent on and influenced by pH and the diol concentration. Accordingly, the pH effect on the present sensing system was investigated in the presence (F) and absence (F0) of 150.0 µM glucose. (Fig. 3 a) shows that a decrease in fluorescence quenching was observed at pH < 7.0, which arises from the facts that the neutral-trigonal boronic acid state was more abundant and the dominant form at low pH and does not bind strongly with the diol moieties of glucose. Furthermore, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond formation between other moieties on the surface of CDs in addition to various ligands on the surface of CDs, could undergo exchanges with highly concentrated protons, leading to particle aggregation and a decreased fluorescence intensity [59,60]. In contrast, an effectively decreased fluorescence intensity was observed at pH > 7.0. At pH values that are too high, lowering the solubility of BS-CDs decreases the fluorescence intensity when a negative charge appears on the surface of the CDs and, according to the deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups, increases the electrostatic repulsion, hence accelerating the decay rate of a chemical in its excited state and decreasing the fluorescence intensity [61]. As a compromise between these two effects, the optimum pH with the highest fluorescence emission was observed when the reaction pH was 7.5, which can be used for glucose sensing by the present BS-CDs receptor.

Another study was conducted to optimize the time required for the reaction, which influences the quenching of the fluorescence of BS-CDs (Fig. 3b). The maximum quenching fluorescence was attained at 30-min intervals of the interaction at several concentrations with a wide range of glucose. The system is useful because it requires less time than other sensing methods described elsewhere [62]. (Fig. S1) describes the practical principle of BS-CDs binding to a glucose molecule. The binding event and associated electronic and steric changes around the boronic acid receptor cause a change in fluorescence emission, which can be correlated with the glucose concentrations as a quencher.

The validity of the synthesized BS-CDs for glucose sensing under optimized circumstances was determined. Glucose addition to boronic acid-functionalized BS-CDs causes considerable changes in the fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. 4 ). As the concentration of glucose is increased, the fluorescence quenching increases, while a nonfluorescent complex forms between the fluorescent BS-CDs and glucose. The inset TEM shows the initial dispersed C-dots aggregate together and form a large assembly (Fig. 4). The absorbance of BS-CDs alone and when glucose is added is compared and there is a large different between them as shown in (Fig. S2) which confirm formation of an assemble. Because each glucose molecule has two pairs of cis-diols, it can covalently bridge the BS-CDs and form large assemblies. The association of fluorophore probe in BS-CDs and glucose as a quencher leads to forming a non-emissive complex. This means that the BS-CDs prob does not participate any more to the fluorescence signal and therefore, the fluorescence intensity be the product of static quenching contributions. The theory and equation of the variation in fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime as a function of added quencher was well explained by Otto Stern and Max Volmer before one century (1919), as molecular species able to deactivate the electronic excited-state of the fluorophore back to its electronic ground state [63,64]. This relation was represented by the Stern-Volmer equation as follows:
F o
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where KSV is the static quenching constant (the Stern-Volmer constant that in a purely dynamic fluorescence quenching process is the product of the molecular quenching rate constant and the fluorescence life time in absence of added quencher), Fo and F are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of glucose as a quencher respectively, and [Q] is glucose quencher concentration [65,66]. The relationship between Fo–F and the concentration of glucose within the range of (1–250) μM are described in Fig. S3, which shows good linearity and coefficient of determination R2 reach to 0.996. The determined detection limit was 0.57 μM according to the standard deviation rule, 3σ/S, where σ describes the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 3) and S represents the slope of the calibration curve.

The efficiency of the fluorescent chemosensor was evaluated according to its selectivity. The effects of different possible and expected species that may either positively or negatively interfere with the present sensing method are investigated in this study. The specific reaction and affinity of boronic acid to the cis-diol fraction of glucose should be free from interferences. These effects arise from the fact that fluorescence deactivation of BS-CDs relies mainly on the complex interactions of glucose with cis-diols with boronic acid groups. However, these functional groups cannot be found in many other substances to cause such interferences [67,68]. Nevertheless, the influence of the potential interfering compounds that normally exist in saliva, such as glutathione, uric acid, cysteine, K+, Cl-, and PO4 3-, on the response of BS-CDs fluorescence was investigated. Figure S4 shows the obtained results, which demonstrate that the effects of these compounds, either in combination or individually, on the developed sensing system are slight, which agrees with the above results. In another interference study, the fluorescence signals of BS-CDs were examined according to the influence of glucose analogues viz. fructose, lactose, maltose, and sucrose. These analogues were introduced individually to the sensing procedure under optimum reaction conditions, and then, the fluorescence intensity of the systems was estimated. In another study, the impact of these four analogous sugars on fluorescence quenching was negligible, which confirms that BS-CDs can identify glucose in the presence of other saccharides. The stereo structure characteristics of glucose and the identification principle of the existing sensing method provided good selectivity. Unlike glucose, there is no further cis conformational diol unit in these four analogous sugars. Thus, they cannot crosslink with BS-CDs and cause similar fluorescent quenching. Table 1 describes the maximum allowable concentrations of the possible interference substances and their effect in the presence of 140 µM glucose. The recovery measurements show that the method is highly selective. The present method provides good and practical results in terms of linearity, detection limits, and analysis time and enriches the applications of carbon dots for glucose sensing compared to previous published research researches by using different nano-sensors Table 2 .

Evaluation of the reliability and analytical applicability of the developed sensing system was performed by testing the glucose levels in saliva for disease diagnosis. Two volunteers who did not suffer from diabetes or prediabetes were enrolled in this test study, both aged >20 years. The individuals were advised to fast for at least 10 hr overnight. The following morning, saliva samples were collected from each in the fasted state for analysis by both the proposed method and a reference (glucose oxidase method using glucose oxidase kit GOD-POD, glucose oxidase–peroxidase) method.

The salivary glucose levels were measured according to the described procedure under optimum experimental conditions. The salivary glucose levels of a normal case obtained using the present BS-CDs platform agreed with those obtained from the reference method and coincided with published results [73,74]. The recovery of glucose was measured to be equal to 97.75% after spiking with different glucose concentrations Table 3 . This result demonstrates that BS-CDs is reliable for the potential analysis of glucose in saliva samples.

A new boron-sulfur-doped carbon dot chemosensor was fabricated and developed for glucose detection in saliva samples via the static quenching mechanism of fluorescent light. Additionally, one of the known limitations of glucose sensing procedures is the usually low LOD, which do not allow its use for blood glucose direct sensing. To compensate for this limitation we propose this sensing methodology for the analysis of glucose in saliva where the concentration of glucose is much higher. The significance of the method is twofold. First, a simple hydrothermal single-step method was adopted in the synthesis process without the need for surface functionalization and immobilization steps. The designed sensor presents strong selectivity and sensitivity and a wide linear range (1–250 μM), in addition to the high stability, eco-friendliness, low cost and biocompatibility of nanoprobes in comparison with the unstable enzymatic procedure. Second, the as-prepared BS-CDs sensor was used to estimate glucose levels in an oral fluid (saliva), which can be considered a clinical diagnostic tool for diabetes. The present measurement method not only provides a methodology for the fabrication of a new chemosensor platform but also greatly improves its feasibility and applicability for the determination of salivary glucose levels.
No funding was received for this work.

We confirm that we have given due consideration to the protection of intellectual property associated with this work and that there are no impediments to publication, including the timing of publication, with respect to intellectual property. In so doing we confirm that we have followed the regulations of our institutions concerning intellectual property.

ce:list-item.ce:para: We further confirm that any aspect of the work covered in this manuscript that has involved human patients has been conducted with the ethical approval of all relevant bodies and that such approvals are acknowledged within the manuscript.
IRB approval was obtained (required for studies and series of 3 or more cases.
Written consent to publish potentially identifying information, such as details or the case, was obtained from the patient(s) or their legal guardian(s).

ce:section.ce:para: The International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE) recommends that authorship be based on the following four criteria:
1. Substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work; AND
2. Drafting the work or revising it critically for important intellectual content; AND
3. Final approval of the version to be published; AND
4. Agreement to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.
All those designated as authors should meet all four criteria for authorship, and all who meet the four criteria should be identified as authors.
-All listed authors meet the ICMJE criteria. We attest that all authors contributed significantly to the creation of this manuscript, each having fulfilled criteria as established by the ICMJE.
-We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors.
-We confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all named authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.105919.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time continuous glucose monitoring system for treatment of diabetes: a systematic review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.B. Hoeks, W.L. Greven, H.W. de Valk, Real-time continuous glucose monitoring system for treatment of diabetes: a systematic review, Diabet. Med. 28 (2011), 386-394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Arylboronic acids: a diabetic eye on glucose sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of 3-amino phenylboronic acid interactions with selected sugars by optical methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kur, M. Przybyt, E. Miller, Study of 3-aminophenyl boronic acid interactions with selected sugars by optical methods, J. Lumin. 183 (2017), 486-493.

G. P. C. Mello , Eliana F. C. Simoes, Diana M. A. Cristaa , Joao M. M. Leitao , Luıs Pinto da Silva , and Joaquim C. G. Esteves da Silva, Glucose Sensing by Fluorescent Nanomaterials, Critical reviews in analytical chemistry, (2019).

Şener, Enhancing biosensor properties of conducting polymers via copolymerization: Synthesis of EDOT-substitutedbis (2-pyridylimino) isoindolato-palladium complex and electrochemical sensing of glucose by its copolymerized film, Biosens. Bioelectron. 87 (2017), 81-88.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, Y. Chen, Z. Liu, Boronate affinity materials for separation and molecular recognition: structure, properties and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015), 8097-8123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The diagnostic applications of saliva— a review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing the general time scale question of boronic acid binding with sugars in aqueous solution at physiological pH
